title: RBA and APRA put banks on 'war footing'
full_text:
The nation's top financial regulators have warned banks they are operating in a new era of heightened geopolitical risk, urging them to bolster their defences against foreign interference and cyberattacks.
The prudential regulator has written to all banks, insurers and super funds outlining its higher expectations around their preparedness to defend the financial system from risks including disinformation campaigns and insider threats to steal information.
Banks are being told to step up their vetting of senior staff, while directors are urged to better integrate geopolitical risk into governance and crisis management frameworks to ensure banks can help the country navigate the most volatile international arena in decades.
APRA chairman John Lonsdale and RBA assistant governor Brad Jones gave co-ordinated speeches at an Australian Banking Association conference in Melbourne on Wednesday, attended by ANZ chief executive Nuno Matos, who is also chair of the ABA, and Westpac CEO Anthony Miller.
They were told new structural risks were emerging from outside the financial system, and Lonsdale said that while most banks were alert to geopolitical risk, "awareness has not always been matched by practical action".
Pointing to "intensifying strategic competition, more frequent grey-zone activity and mounting strain on the rules-based international order", Lonsdale said responses would require "a substantial uplift in strategic investment, workforce capability and integration across security operations".
Jones said new non-financial risks included asset expropriation - where governments or public agencies seize privately owned assets or property - and techniques to evade sanctions screening. Regulators are also worried about "co-ordination failure across industry and official sector agencies in a 'poly crisis' scenario if multiple risks were to crystallise simultaneously", he said.
With the arrival of powerful artificial intelligence models including Anthropic's Mythos rattling the banks, the regulators reiterated cybersecurity would remain a heightened threat.
"Defensive AI capabilities have become a core pillar of resilience planning," Jones said.
APRA will also write to select group of larger organisations with heightened exposure to geopolitical shocks and ask them to undertake "targeted readiness assessments". The regulator also wants smaller institutions to lift their game.
ANZ's Matos said warfare and economic nationalism were challenging the rules-based order that defined much of the post-World War era. "At the geopolitical level, power politics, instead of values, is resurgent," he said.
As the Department of Home Affairs warns Australia is the target of sophisticated and persistent foreign interference activities from a range of countries, Jones said managing the threat would require "stronger vetting processes, including at senior levels, a tightening in access to sensitive information, increased demand for security clearances, and the use of sophisticated behavioural analytics". He also called for more work on defending the payments system, should it be taken out in a war.
"It is critical that contingency planning efforts are directed at maintaining a minimal level of service provision in periods of stress," he said.
APRA, the RBA and the banks have been working for several years with the national security agencies and Five Eyes partners overseas.
Banks are also involved in the Systems of National Significance, a group of 170 critical infrastructure providers across the economy.
Australian Banking Association chief executive Simon Birmingham said the major banks were prioritising investment in understanding geopolitical risk and building resilience. "Banks have a role to ensure their resilience against threats and also to strengthen the nation by ensuring Australia is as economically strong as possible."
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: James Eyers
heading: Banking and Economy
